_note: Ernest error_correction batch 1 — 15 items at B1, weighted toward his measured weak patterns: articles + uncountable nouns (5 items), conditionals (3, will/would Russian-L1 traps), tenses (3, present perfect + continuous), phrasal verbs / calques (2), word choice / preposition errors (2). Themes: school, basketball, friends, sports, books. Single-attempt scoring; player types corrected sentence or just the fix.

exercises.incorrect_sentence: He made a good progress in maths this term.
exercises.reasoning: 'Progress' is uncountable — no 'a/an', even with an adjective. Russian 'прогресс' is countable, so 'a good progress' is a direct calque. ✓ 'made good progress' / 'made significant progress'.

exercises.incorrect_sentence: I need an information about the bus schedule.
exercises.reasoning: 'Information' is uncountable — no 'a/an', no plural. Russian 'информация' can feel countable, so 'an information' is a calque trap. ✓ 'some information' or 'a piece of information'.

exercises.incorrect_sentence: We have a basketball match the next Friday.
exercises.reasoning: next/last + day = no article. ✗ 'the next Friday'. ✓ 'next Friday' / 'last Saturday'.

exercises.incorrect_sentence: My brother is engineer at a tech company.
exercises.reasoning: Professions take 'a/an' — classifying A. ✗ 'is engineer' (Russian 'Он инженер' has no article). ✓ 'is an engineer' / 'is a teacher'.

exercises.incorrect_sentence: We need to take an action immediately.
exercises.reasoning: 'Take action' is a fixed phrase — no article. ✗ 'take an action' / 'take the action'. Other zero-article fixed phrases: 'make progress', 'give advice'.

exercises.incorrect_sentence: If it will rain tomorrow, we won't play basketball.
exercises.reasoning: 1st conditional: present simple in the if-clause, will in the main clause. ✗ 'if it will rain' (Russian 'если будет дождь' uses future). ✓ 'if it rains, we won't play'.

exercises.incorrect_sentence: If I would have more time, I would read more books.
exercises.reasoning: 2nd conditional: past simple in the if-clause, would in the main clause. ✗ 'if I would have' (Russian 'если бы я бы' double-conditional calque). ✓ 'if I had more time, I would read more'.

exercises.incorrect_sentence: If you will study hard, you will pass the exam.
exercises.reasoning: 1st conditional: present in the if-clause, will in the main clause. ✓ 'if you study hard, you will pass'.
Life experience without specific time → present perfect: 'have been to + place'. ✗ 'I was to London' (Russian 'был в Лондоне' translates literally as past simple). ✓ 'I have been to London twice'.

exercises.incorrect_sentence: I read a really good book right now.
exercises.reasoning: 'Right now' → present continuous, not present simple. ✗ 'I read right now' (Russian 'я читаю' covers both 'I read' habitually and 'I am reading' now). ✓ 'I am reading a good book right now'.

exercises.incorrect_sentence: I see this film three times.
exercises.reasoning: Repeated experience up to now → present perfect: 'have seen X times'. ✗ 'I see this film three times' (Russian 'я смотрел три раза' = past simple, but English uses present perfect for unfinished time). ✓ 'I have seen this film three times'.

exercises.incorrect_sentence: Don't forget to close the lights when you leave.
exercises.reasoning: Russian 'выключить свет' / 'погасить свет' calques to 'close the lights', but English uses 'turn off'. ✓ 'turn off the lights' / 'switch off the lights'. (Logged in your coach notes from the previous session.)

exercises.incorrect_sentence: I need to finish my homeworks before training.
exercises.reasoning: 'Homework' is uncountable — no plural 's'. ✗ 'homeworks' doesn't exist. ✓ 'homework' / 'a lot of homework' / 'two hours of homework'.

exercises.incorrect_sentence: I was waiting my friend at the gym for 20 minutes.
exercises.reasoning: 'Wait' takes 'for' before the object. ✗ 'waiting my friend' (Russian 'ждать кого-то' has no preposition). ✓ 'waiting for my friend'.

exercises.incorrect_sentence: We arrived to the gym late for training.
exercises.reasoning: 'Arrive at' (specific place / event) or 'arrive in' (city / country). ✗ 'arrived to' (Russian 'приехать в' calque). ✓ 'arrived at the gym' / 'arrived in London'.
